title: Day 14 Interview Prep
description: Cumulative Week 2 interview practice — APIs, hooks, Context, routing, performance, event loop.

questions.question: Walk through your Machine Coding Round 2 architecture in two minutes.
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Can you articulate structure under pressure?
questions.sampleAnswer: I scaffolded features/products and features/cart. CartProvider wraps BrowserRouter at root. useDebounce handles search. useMemo derives filtered sorted products. Cart uses useReducer with ADD_ITEM, REMOVE_ITEM, UPDATE_QUANTITY. Types in src/types. API layer separate from components.

questions.question: Explain debouncing and how you implemented it this week.
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Day 09 core — must own this after Week 2.
keyPoints: Fetch on debounced value not raw input
questions.sampleAnswer: Debouncing delays API calls until user stops typing. useDebounce holds debouncedValue updated via setTimeout in useEffect with cleanup. Search input updates immediately; fetch effect depends on debouncedSearch. Prevents request storm and race conditions.

questions.question: Why Context + useReducer for the cart instead of prop drilling?
keyPoints: Cart needed on listing, cart page, navbar
Redux is Week 3 — Context sufficient today
questions.sampleAnswer: Cart state is needed on product listing, cart page, and navbar badge — three branches of the tree. Prop drilling passes handlers through intermediate components that do not use cart. Context + useReducer gives any component dispatch and state without drilling.

questions.question: Explain microtasks vs macrotasks with a code example.
keyPoints: Connect to debounce and fetch ordering
questions.sampleAnswer: After sync code, all microtasks run — Promise.then, queueMicrotask. Then one macrotask — setTimeout, I/O. Example: log 1, setTimeout log 4, Promise log 3, log 2 → output 1, 2, 3, 4. Debounce timer is macrotask; fetch resolution is microtask.

questions.question: How do you prevent unnecessary re-renders in a product listing?
questions.sampleAnswer: I memo ProductCard so unchanged products skip re-render when filter state changes. useMemo caches filtered sorted list. Keys use product.id. I avoid inline arrow props that break memo. I verify with React DevTools highlight updates — optimize only what profiling shows.

questions.question: How would you add /products/:productId to your Round 2 app?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Day 13 dynamic routing applied to e-commerce.
questions.sampleAnswer: Add route products/:productId with ProductDetailPage. useParams<{ productId: string }>(). useEffect fetches when productId changes. ProductCard Link to `/products/${product.id}`. API 404 shows Not Found. Optional chaining on nullable fields.

questions.question: You have 30 minutes left in machine coding and cart is broken. Prioritize — what do you do?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Time management and debugging under pressure.
keyPoints: Communicate trade-offs aloud in real interview
questions.sampleAnswer: I strip cart to ADD_ITEM only — add button dispatches, cart page shows items and total. Verify CartProvider wraps router. Console.log reducer output. Skip quantity controls temporarily. Fix build errors before CSS. In real interview I narrate: 'Cart add works; quantity next if time permits.'
followUps: When ask interviewer for scope cut?

questions.question: Compare your Week 2 stack to Redux Toolkit. When would you reach for RTK?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Bridge to Week 3 without knowing RTK deeply yet.
keyPoints: Context + reducer sufficient for small apps
RTK for large teams, DevTools, middleware
questions.sampleAnswer: My Week 2 cart uses Context + useReducer — fine for one feature, one app size. RTK when multiple global slices, middleware for logging/auth, Redux DevTools at scale, or RTK Query replacing manual fetch/cache. I would not add Redux to Round 2 — over-engineering under time pressure.
